h1Title: Adult Hypertrophic Pyloric Stenosis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Adult hypertrophic pyloric stenosis is a rare form of gastric outlet obstruction caused by hypertrophy and hyperplasia of the circular muscle of the pylorus, leading to progressive narrowing of the pyloric channel in adulthood. Unlike infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis — which affects 2-4 per 1,000 live births and is one of the most common surgical emergencies in neonates — the adult form is an uncommon condition with fewer than 300 well-documented cases reported in the surgical literature. It presents with postprandial nausea, vomiting, early satiety, and epigastric pain. The diagnosis is established endoscopically and radiologically and requires exclusion of peptic ulcer disease and malignancy. Treatment is surgical in most cases, with laparoscopic or open pyloromyotomy or pyloroplasty achieving excellent outcomes.

keyStats.prevalence: Fewer than 300 well-documented adult cases in the English-language surgical literature; true incidence unknown but estimated at less than 1 in 100,000 adults per year
keyStats.demographics: Described across all adult age groups with a bimodal distribution in some series: patients in the third decade (likely unrecognized congenital disease) and patients in the fifth to seventh decades (secondary hypertrophy); slight male predominance reported in most series
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at diagnosis in published series approximately 45-55 years; a secondary peak in infancy resolves but may re-emerge in adults with longstanding peptic disease
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global case count unavailable; sporadic case reports from Europe, North America, Asia, and India; no reliable epidemiological cohort studies due to extreme rarity

definition: Adult hypertrophic pyloric stenosis (ICD-10: K31.1) is a structural disorder of the pylorus characterized by circular-muscle hypertrophy and submucosal fibrosis that narrows the pyloric channel and obstructs gastric emptying in patients past infancy. The condition is classified as primary (idiopathic) when no preceding disease is identified — with many such cases likely representing adult expression of a congenital defect that was subclinical in infancy — and secondary when pyloric muscle hypertrophy develops as a consequence of longstanding peptic ulcer disease, gastritis, or neoplastic infiltration of the pyloric region. Histologically, the hypertrophied pylorus shows increased muscle fiber diameter, submucosal edema, and fibrosis, closely resembling the infantile variant. The resulting gastric outlet obstruction leads to progressive gastric distension, acid hypersecretion, vomiting of undigested food and gastric contents, and consequent hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis with hypokalemia — the same electrolyte derangement seen in newborns with this condition. Gastroenterology and upper gastrointestinal surgery share management of this rare condition; accurate diagnosis requires upper GI endoscopy, barium meal, and cross-sectional imaging to exclude peptic ulcer stenosis and gastric or duodenal cancer as more common causes of the same presentation.

typesClassification.description: Occurs without antecedent peptic disease or other identifiable cause. Many cases may represent late presentation of congenital hypertrophy that was asymptomatic in infancy and childhood, becoming clinically apparent as the pyloric hypertrophy progresses in adulthood.

Pyloric muscle hypertrophy occurring as a response to chronic pyloric irritation and scarring from peptic ulcer disease, Helicobacter pylori gastritis, or neoplastic infiltration. The hypertrophy is the downstream consequence of longstanding pyloric injury rather than a primary structural anomaly.

primarySymptoms: Progressive postprandial nausea and vomiting of undigested or partially digested food, occurring one to three hours after eating, often projectile in nature — the hallmark symptom of gastric outlet obstruction regardless of cause.
Early satiety and rapid sensation of fullness after only a small amount of food, reflecting reduced gastric emptying through the narrowed pylorus.
Epigastric fullness, bloating, and discomfort throughout the day that worsens after meals and partially improves after vomiting.
Weight loss, sometimes substantial (5-15 kg over months), from reduced caloric intake secondary to pain-avoidance and early satiety.
Visible gastric peristalsis — a wave-like epigastric movement — occasionally observed in thin patients with significant gastric distension.
Dehydration, dry mucous membranes, and reduced urine output from repeated vomiting of large fluid volumes.
Epigastric pain or burning, particularly in cases with secondary peptic ulcer disease as the underlying cause of pyloric hypertrophy.
A succession splash (a sloshing sound on shaking the abdomen) audible up to four to six hours after the last meal, indicating retained gastric contents from delayed emptying.
Fatigue, muscle cramps, and palpitations in patients with prolonged vomiting and resultant hypokalemia from loss of gastric hydrochloric acid.
Regurgitation of bile-stained fluid is notably absent in pure gastric outlet obstruction because the pyloric obstruction prevents duodenal bile from refluxing into the stomach.
earlyWarningSigns: Gradual worsening of postprandial nausea over weeks to months in a middle-aged adult without prior gastrointestinal diagnosis — early gastric outlet obstruction before vomiting becomes projectile.
Increasing avoidance of solid foods with preference for liquids only, because liquid food passes more easily through a narrowed pylorus.
Unexplained weight loss of more than 5% body weight over three months in a patient with epigastric symptoms, warranting prompt upper GI investigation.
New onset of visible epigastric distension that is relieved by vomiting in a patient with known longstanding peptic ulcer disease.
emergencySigns: Inability to tolerate any oral intake combined with severe dehydration, hypotension, and tachycardia — acute gastric outlet obstruction requiring intravenous fluid resuscitation and nasogastric decompression before investigation.

Severe hypokalemia (potassium below 2.5 mmol/L) and metabolic alkalosis (pH above 7.55 or bicarbonate above 35 mmol/L) causing cardiac arrhythmias or profound muscle weakness — requires urgent electrolyte correction.
New hematemesis or coffee-ground vomiting in a patient with longstanding pyloric symptoms — signals active peptic ulceration or mucosal injury requiring urgent endoscopy and exclusion of malignancy.
Peritonitis or free air under the diaphragm on plain chest radiograph in a patient with gastric outlet symptoms — gastric perforation requires emergency surgery.

causes.cause: Congenital pyloric hypertrophy with late presentation
causes.description: Many primary adult cases are considered to be the adult expression of congenital pyloric muscle hypertrophy that was subclinical or borderline in infancy. With longitudinal growth and additional stimuli (gastric acid hypersecretion, dietary factors), the pyloric muscle becomes progressively more obstructive until it causes symptoms in adulthood.

causes.cause: Chronic peptic ulcer disease with pyloric scarring
causes.description: Repeated cycles of peptic ulceration and healing in the prepyloric region and duodenal bulb cause inflammatory edema, fibrosis, and secondary smooth muscle hypertrophy. Over years this produces the radiological and endoscopic appearance of hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, though secondary fibrosis rather than primary hypertrophy is the dominant mechanism.

causes.cause: Helicobacter pylori gastritis with antral involvement
causes.description: H. pylori infection driving chronic antral and pyloric gastritis is proposed as a trigger for smooth muscle hypertrophy in susceptible individuals. Eradication of H. pylori may improve symptoms in some secondary cases.

causes.cause: Neoplastic infiltration of the pyloric region
causes.description: Gastric antral carcinoma, lymphoma, or metastatic cancer infiltrating the submucosa and muscle layers of the pylorus can produce a hypertrophic pyloric stenosis-like appearance endoscopically and radiologically. Histological sampling is mandatory to exclude this in all adult cases.

causes.cause: Idiopathic smooth muscle hypertrophy without identified etiology
causes.description: A proportion of adult cases, particularly in younger patients without peptic history, have no identifiable cause on detailed investigation. Hormonal factors (gastrin hypersecretion stimulating circular muscle hypertrophy) and genetic susceptibility analogous to the familial clustering seen in infantile pyloric stenosis are proposed but unproven mechanisms.

riskFactors.factor: History of infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis
riskFactors.description: Adults who had surgically corrected infantile pyloric stenosis may have residual or recurrent pyloric hypertrophy. Cases of recurrent symptomatic stenosis years after pyloromyotomy in childhood are documented in surgical literature.

Chronic peptic ulceration in the pyloric channel or duodenal bulb is the most common identifiable risk factor for secondary adult hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, through repeated scarring and secondary muscle hypertrophy.
H. pylori-associated chronic antral gastritis may predispose to pyloric smooth-muscle hypertrophy. H. pylori testing and eradication are standard in all patients investigated for pyloric disease.
Most published series show slight male predominance, consistent with the male predominance in both infantile pyloric stenosis and peptic ulcer disease.
Hypergastrinemia from a gastrinoma chronically stimulates gastric acid secretion and may drive pyloric smooth muscle hypertrophy as a secondary phenomenon.

Anastomotic scarring after prior gastric surgery, Billroth procedures, or inadvertent pyloric injury can lead to secondary stenosis that may be histologically indistinguishable from hypertrophic pyloric stenosis on endoscopic biopsy.

affectedDemographics: Adults of all ages from the late teens onwards; some series show a bimodal peak in the third decade and fifth to seventh decades.
Slight male predominance in published case series, paralleling the strong male predominance in infantile pyloric stenosis.
Patients with a history of peptic ulcer disease or prior gastric surgery are over-represented in secondary pyloric stenosis.
No clear geographic or ethnic clustering in the sparse published literature; cases reported from North America, Europe, Asia, and India.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with a careful history eliciting the progression of postprandial vomiting, weight loss, and food intolerance. Upper GI endoscopy is the first-line investigation: the endoscopist typically encounters a markedly narrowed pyloric channel that resists passage of a standard adult endoscope, with a puckered or fixed pyloric opening surrounded by normal or slightly erythematous mucosa — the characteristic 'cervix sign' described in the older literature. Biopsy of the pyloric mucosa is mandatory at endoscopy to exclude malignancy; a negative biopsy does not exclude submucosal or muscular neoplasm, and multiple deep biopsies or bite-on-bite technique is preferred. Barium meal or upper GI fluoroscopy demonstrates a long, narrow elongated pyloric channel (the 'string sign'), delayed gastric emptying, and peristaltic waves that are unable to empty the stomach — similar to infantile pyloric stenosis. Abdominal ultrasound can measure the pyloric muscle thickness (normal adult threshold approximately 3 mm; hypertrophy defined as greater than 5 mm in most adult series) and is non-invasive. CT of the abdomen with oral and intravenous contrast images the hypertrophied pyloric muscle, excludes peri-pyloric lymphadenopathy, masses, and local invasion, and should always be performed in adults to exclude gastric cancer as the cause. Serum gastrin levels and 13C-urea breath test for H. pylori complete the baseline workup. Examination under anesthesia with intraoperative ultrasound is sometimes used to plan the operative approach.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly visualizes the narrowed pyloric channel; excludes peptic ulcer, malignancy, and other structural lesions; biopsy samples confirm or exclude carcinoma
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fasting for six hours before the procedure. An endoscope is passed via the throat with throat spray or light sedation. Mild bloating during the 15-20-minute procedure. Recovery within one to two hours. Biopsy results take two to five days.

diagnosticTests.test: Barium upper GI swallow and meal fluoroscopy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates elongated pyloric channel, delayed gastric emptying, peristaltic wave pattern, and gastric distension — provides functional as well as anatomical assessment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fasting overnight. Patient drinks a chalky barium liquid and assumes various positions while X-ray images are taken over 30-60 minutes. No radiation risk beyond that of standard abdominal X-rays. Results read immediately by the radiologist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive measurement of pyloric muscle thickness; supports diagnosis when muscle hypertrophy is demonstrated (greater than 5 mm in adults)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Gel applied to the upper abdomen; the probe is pressed gently in the right upper quadrant. Takes 15-20 minutes. No preparation needed unless combined with other abdominal imaging.

diagnosticTests.test: CT abdomen and pelvis with oral and IV contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes gastric cancer and peri-pyloric lymphadenopathy; images the extent of pyloric hypertrophy and any secondary pathology
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Intravenous contrast injection and oral contrast ingestion before scanning. Takes 10-15 minutes in the scanner. Results reviewed by a radiologist within 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum gastrin level and 13C-urea breath test for H. pylori
diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated fasting gastrin suggests Zollinger-Ellison syndrome; H. pylori breath test identifies a potentially treatable secondary cause
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Gastrin is a fasting blood test requiring cessation of proton pump inhibitors for two weeks. Breath test involves swallowing a capsule or liquid and breathing into a collection bag after 30 minutes. Both tests are outpatient and same-day.

diagnosticTests.test: Electrolytes, renal function, and arterial blood gas
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis and hypokalemia from vomiting; guides pre-operative resuscitation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw, with an optional arterial blood gas from the wrist or femoral artery. Results within one to two hours; critical values prompt immediate management.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of adult hypertrophic pyloric stenosis is primarily surgical after adequate pre-operative optimization. Most patients present with varying degrees of dehydration, metabolic alkalosis, and hypokalemia from chronic vomiting; intravenous normal saline supplemented with potassium chloride restores electrolyte balance and must precede any surgical intervention — typically requiring 24-72 hours. Nasogastric drainage relieves gastric distension, allows mucosal inflammation to settle, and improves operative visibility. H. pylori eradication and proton pump inhibitor therapy are initiated when H. pylori is positive or peptic disease is identified as a contributing cause. Endoscopic balloon dilation of the pyloric channel has been reported in small series as a minimally invasive alternative, offering transient relief in poor surgical candidates; however, dilation of a hypertrophied muscle provides only modest and often short-lived improvement compared with surgical correction. The standard surgical procedures are pyloromyotomy (Ramstedt-type longitudinal myotomy extended to the duodenal mucosa, as in the infant procedure) or pyloroplasty (Heineke-Mikulicz or Finney pyloroplasty), which widens the pyloric channel by dividing and rearranging the muscle transversally. Both procedures are achievable laparoscopically in experienced centers with comparable outcomes to open surgery. Pyloroplasty is preferred when the pyloric channel has been shortened and fibrosed by peptic disease, as it addresses the mucosal redundancy as well as the muscular obstruction. Gastrojejunostomy (creating a bypass from the stomach to the jejunum) is reserved for cases where local disease makes the pylorus unsuitable for direct repair — particularly in secondary stenosis with extensive scarring or when cancer cannot definitively be excluded intraoperatively.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous saline (0.9% NaCl) with potassium chloride supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Essential pre-operative resuscitation for all patients presenting with gastric outlet obstruction from any cause. Replaces the chloride and potassium lost through vomiting of gastric acid, correcting hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis before surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Electrolyte normalization within 24-72 hours in most patients; failure to correct metabolic alkalosis pre-operatively increases anaesthetic risk substantially

medicalTreatments.name: Proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole 40 mg or pantoprazole 40 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduce gastric acid hypersecretion, protect the inflamed pyloric mucosa, and treat coexistent peptic ulcer disease. Given pre-operatively and continued for at least eight weeks post-operatively.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in gastric acid burden; do not reverse muscle hypertrophy but reduce secondary mucosal inflammation contributing to obstruction

medicalTreatments.name: H. pylori eradication therapy (standard triple or quadruple therapy based on local resistance patterns)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard first-line eradication when H. pylori is positive; may reduce secondary pyloric inflammation and prevent further peptic-related muscle hypertrophy. Confirmed by repeat breath test six to eight weeks after completion.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: H. pylori eradication rates 85-95% with appropriate regimens; reduction in pyloric obstruction depends on the extent to which active inflammation contributes to stenosis

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic or open pyloromyotomy (extended Ramstedt procedure)
surgicalOptions.description: A longitudinal incision through the pyloric serosa and circular muscle down to but not through the duodenal mucosa, identical in concept to the infant Ramstedt procedure. Relieves the muscular obstruction while preserving mucosal integrity. Preferred for primary (idiopathic) hypertrophy without fibrosis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom resolution in 80-95% in published adult case reports; minimal morbidity when performed by experienced upper GI surgeons

surgicalOptions.name: Heineke-Mikulicz pyloroplasty
surgicalOptions.description: A longitudinal incision through the full thickness of the pyloric channel is closed transversally, widening the pyloric lumen. Used when the pyloric channel is shortened by fibrosis or peptic disease complicates pure myotomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Success rates comparable to pyloromyotomy for secondary pyloric stenosis; slight increase in risk of duodenal leak versus myotomy alone

surgicalOptions.name: Finney pyloroplasty
surgicalOptions.description: A more extensive pyloroplasty that creates a wider opening by incising the distal stomach and proximal duodenum and sewing them together in a side-to-side fashion. Used for long or complex pyloric stenosis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective gastric emptying in over 90% of cases; most appropriate for severely scarred or fibrotic channels where Heineke-Mikulicz is insufficient

surgicalOptions.name: Gastrojejunostomy (bypass procedure)
surgicalOptions.description: The posterior wall of the stomach is anastomosed to a loop of proximal jejunum, bypassing the pylorus entirely. Reserved for cases where the pylorus cannot be directly repaired — extensive malignant infiltration that cannot be excluded, severe scarring, or prior failed pyloroplasty.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reliable long-term gastric emptying; carries risks of afferent loop syndrome, bile reflux gastritis, and marginal ulceration in approximately 10-15% of patients

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic balloon dilation
surgicalOptions.description: A through-the-scope balloon catheter is inflated to 12-20 mm diameter in the pyloric channel under direct vision. A temporizing procedure rather than a cure; suitable for poor surgical candidates or as a bridge to definitive surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Initial symptom relief in 50-70% of cases; durability limited with most patients requiring repeat dilation or eventual surgery within one to two years

alternativeTreatments.name: Proton pump inhibitor therapy as bridge to surgery
alternativeTreatments.description: In patients waiting for elective surgery, high-dose PPI therapy (40 mg twice daily) reduces acid burden, minimizes secondary mucosal edema at the pylorus, and may transiently improve gastric emptying.

whySeeSpecialist: Adult hypertrophic pyloric stenosis should be evaluated jointly by a gastroenterologist for endoscopic assessment and exclusion of peptic and malignant causes, and by an upper GI surgeon experienced in pyloric surgery. Given the extreme rarity, centers with specialist upper GI or bariatric surgery services are preferred.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a gastroenterologist with expertise in upper GI endoscopy and a surgeon with specific experience in pyloric and gastric procedures. A center with multidisciplinary tumor board access is important to definitively exclude gastric malignancy before undertaking benign surgical repair.
hospitalCriteria: Upper GI endoscopy suite with biopsy capability and expert radiology reporting
CT imaging with subspecialized abdominal radiology review
Surgical unit with laparoscopic upper GI surgery expertise
Gastroenterology multidisciplinary team for excluding malignancy
High-dependency unit for pre- and post-operative electrolyte monitoring
keyFacilities: Gastroenterology endoscopy centres with complex pyloric expertise
Radiology departments offering fluoroscopy and CT with subspecialty reporting
preventionStrategies: Treat H. pylori infection promptly when identified to reduce the chronic antral inflammation that may drive secondary pyloric hypertrophy.
Maintain effective peptic ulcer disease management with proton pump inhibitors and H. pylori eradication to prevent repeated pyloric scarring cycles.

Seek upper GI evaluation promptly when postprandial vomiting, early satiety, or unexplained weight loss develops — early diagnosis prevents the severe metabolic derangements of advanced gastric outlet obstruction.
Follow up after childhood pyloromyotomy for infantile pyloric stenosis with clinical awareness that adult recurrence, though rare, has been documented.
lifestyleModifications: Eat small, frequent meals of soft or liquid consistency while awaiting definitive surgery to maximize caloric intake through the partially obstructed pylorus.
Eat slowly and chew food thoroughly; avoid large solid boluses that cannot pass the narrowed pylorus.
Remain upright for at least one hour after meals to use gravity to facilitate gastric emptying.
Avoid NSAIDs, aspirin, and alcohol, which worsen gastric mucosal inflammation and increase secondary pyloric edema.
Monitor weight weekly and report to the surgical team if continuing to lose weight, as this signals inadequate nutritional compensation.
Drink fluids between rather than during meals to maximize solid-food tolerance.

recommended: Liquid and semi-liquid foods (soups, smoothies, liquid nutritional supplements) that pass more easily through the narrowed pylorus
Small portions of finely minced or pureed protein sources (egg, minced chicken, soft fish) for nutritional maintenance
Oral electrolyte solutions (e.g., sports drinks, rehydration sachets) to replace sodium and potassium lost through vomiting
Calorie-dense nutritional supplements (liquid meal replacements) to maintain weight when solid food is poorly tolerated
avoid: Large solid meals that obstruct the narrowed pylorus and provoke vomiting
Fibrous, stringy vegetables and tough meats that resist mechanical digestion
Carbonated drinks and excessive fluids during meals, which add volume without nutrition and precipitate vomiting
Alcohol, aspirin, and NSAIDs, which exacerbate gastric mucosal inflammation

exerciseGuidelines: Gentle walking is safe and encouraged during the pre-operative waiting period. Strenuous exercise should be avoided when the patient is significantly malnourished, dehydrated, or hypokalemic. After surgical repair, light activity resumes within one to two weeks of laparoscopic procedures and two to four weeks after open surgery; full activity by six to eight weeks.
dailyManagement: Take proton pump inhibitors as prescribed before morning meal to maximize gastric acid suppression.
Weigh daily and record food intake; report weight loss exceeding 1 kg per week to the surgical team.
Keep a food diary noting which foods and portion sizes are tolerated to guide the nutrition team.
Maintain adequate oral hydration — at least 1.5 L of fluid per day in small, frequent amounts — to compensate for losses from vomiting.
Attend scheduled outpatient appointments for blood electrolyte monitoring, particularly potassium levels.
After surgery, advance the diet from liquids to soft foods to normal diet as tolerated, guided by the surgical team's instructions.
prognosis: With appropriate surgical treatment, prognosis for primary adult hypertrophic pyloric stenosis is excellent. Pyloromyotomy and pyloroplasty achieve resolution of gastric outlet obstruction and restoration of normal gastric emptying in 80-95% of patients in the published case series, with low operative morbidity when performed electively after pre-operative metabolic correction. Long-term recurrence of pyloric stenosis after successful surgical repair is uncommon in the primary form. Secondary pyloric stenosis related to peptic ulcer disease has a good outcome if H. pylori eradication and acid suppression are maintained and the patient avoids NSAIDs. Cases where malignancy was the unidentified underlying cause have prognosis determined by tumor stage at diagnosis. Nutritional rehabilitation after resolution of gastric outlet obstruction typically takes two to four months; most patients regain lost weight within six months. Unrecognized or untreated adult pyloric stenosis is potentially life-threatening from severe electrolyte derangements, aspiration of retained gastric contents, and malnutrition.
recoveryTimeline: Laparoscopic pyloromyotomy or pyloroplasty: most patients tolerate oral fluids within 24-48 hours and are discharged on day two to three. Diet advances from liquids to normal over two to four weeks. Open procedures require three to five days of hospitalization and four to six weeks for full recovery. Return to normal nutritional status takes six to twelve weeks in malnourished patients.
complications: Duodenal mucosal perforation during pyloromyotomy, occurring in under 3% of cases; recognized intraoperatively and repaired primarily with an excellent outcome if immediate.
Incomplete myotomy with persistent or recurrent gastric outlet obstruction; requires revision surgery or endoscopic dilation in approximately 5% of cases.
Bile reflux gastritis or marginal ulceration after gastrojejunostomy bypass, presenting with epigastric burning and requires long-term PPI therapy.
Gastric stasis (delayed gastric emptying) in the post-operative period, managed conservatively with nasogastric drainage, prokinetic drugs, and nutritional support.
Aspiration pneumonia from vomiting of retained gastric contents before diagnosis or during induction of anesthesia — a preventable complication if pre-operative nasogastric drainage is performed.
Malnutrition and micronutrient deficiency (iron, B12, folate) from prolonged inadequate oral intake before diagnosis; corrected with dietary rehabilitation and supplementation after surgery.

supportResources.name: Society of American Gastrointestinal and Endoscopic Surgeons (SAGES)
supportResources.description: Professional society for minimally invasive GI surgery, including laparoscopic pyloric procedures; provides a surgeon-finder tool for patients seeking expert upper GI surgeons.

supportResources.name: American College of Gastroenterology — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: ACG patient information on upper GI diseases, including stomach disorders, from the leading US gastroenterology professional organization.

supportResources.name: NHS — Gastric Outlet Obstruction Patient Information
supportResources.description: UK NHS patient information on stomach and pyloric conditions; useful for understanding the investigations and treatment pathway for gastric outlet obstruction in an NHS setting.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peptic ulcer disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peptic ulcer disease is the most common cause of gastric outlet obstruction in adults and closely mimics adult hypertrophic pyloric stenosis both clinically and endoscopically. Ulcer-related stenosis shows mucosal ulceration, deformity, and scarring on endoscopy and barium meal, whereas true hypertrophic stenosis shows an elongated smooth pyloric channel without mucosal break. Histology and endoscopic appearance together differentiate them, though the two may coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gastroesophageal reflux disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gastroesophageal reflux causes heartburn, regurgitation of acid, and occasionally vomiting, but without the gastric distension, succession splash, or projectile postprandial vomiting of outlet obstruction. GERD symptoms are typically worst when lying down; pyloric stenosis vomiting relates specifically to the timing of meals and food load.

confusedWithConditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IBS produces variable abdominal pain with altered bowel habit — constipation, diarrhea, or both — without the structural gastric outlet obstruction, projectile vomiting of undigested food, or objective electrolyte derangements seen in pyloric stenosis. Upper GI endoscopy and imaging are normal in IBS.

relatedConditions.name: Peptic ulcer disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic peptic ulceration is the most common predisposing cause of secondary adult hypertrophic pyloric stenosis and must be excluded in every adult presenting with pyloric channel narrowing.

relatedConditions.name: Gastroesophageal reflux disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Gastric outlet obstruction from pyloric stenosis can cause elevated intragastric pressure that worsens or mimics GERD; the two conditions are managed together in some patients.

relatedConditions.name: Alcohol-induced chronic pancreatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic pancreatitis can rarely infiltrate the duodenal wall and produce duodenal obstruction that mimics pyloric stenosis; pancreatic imaging is part of the workup for atypical cases.

relatedConditions.name: Colorectal cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Gastric and duodenal malignancy must be excluded in all adult cases of apparent hypertrophic pyloric stenosis; biopsy sampling at endoscopy is mandatory.

faqs.question: What is adult hypertrophic pyloric stenosis?
faqs.answer: Adult hypertrophic pyloric stenosis is a rare condition in which the circular muscle around the pylorus — the junction between the stomach and the small intestine — becomes thickened and narrows the passage, causing gastric outlet obstruction. It presents with postprandial vomiting, early satiety, and weight loss. Unlike the infant form, it is uncommon, with fewer than 300 documented adult cases in the medical literature.

faqs.question: What causes pyloric stenosis in adults?
faqs.answer: In adults, pyloric stenosis may be primary (idiopathic, possibly a late presentation of congenital hypertrophy) or secondary to chronic peptic ulcer disease and scarring, H. pylori gastritis, or neoplastic infiltration. Many cases likely represent longstanding congenital hypertrophy that became symptomatic in adulthood rather than a new-onset adult disease.

faqs.question: How is adult pyloric stenosis different from the infant form?
faqs.answer: Infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis affects 2-4 in 1,000 newborns in the first four to six weeks of life with projectile non-bilious vomiting and is treated by Ramstedt pyloromyotomy with a near-100% cure rate. The adult form is rare, may be primary or secondary to peptic disease, and requires exclusion of malignancy before surgery. The surgical principles are similar but adult presentation is more variable.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of pyloric stenosis in adults?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are postprandial nausea and vomiting of undigested food one to three hours after eating, early satiety, epigastric bloating, and progressive weight loss. A succession splash (sloshing sound when the abdomen is shaken) indicates retained gastric contents. In severe cases, repeated vomiting leads to dehydration and low potassium levels.

faqs.question: How is adult hypertrophic pyloric stenosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Upper GI endoscopy is the first-line investigation, typically showing a narrowed, elongated pyloric channel. Barium meal demonstrates the classical string sign of a long narrow pylorus with delayed gastric emptying. CT of the abdomen excludes malignancy. Blood tests confirm the metabolic alkalosis and low potassium from vomiting. Abdominal ultrasound can measure pyloric muscle thickness.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for adult pyloric stenosis?
faqs.answer: Surgery is the definitive treatment. After pre-operative fluid and electrolyte correction, laparoscopic or open pyloromyotomy divides the thickened muscle without entering the bowel. Pyloroplasty widens the pyloric channel by incising and suturing it transversally. Gastrojejunostomy (stomach-to-small-intestine bypass) is reserved for complex or recurrent cases. Endoscopic balloon dilation offers temporary relief in poor surgical candidates.

faqs.question: Can pyloric stenosis in adults be treated without surgery?
faqs.answer: Endoscopic balloon dilation can provide symptomatic relief in patients unfit for surgery and has been used as a bridge to definitive repair. Medical treatment with proton pump inhibitors reduces acid and secondary pyloric inflammation but does not reverse muscle hypertrophy. H. pylori eradication may improve secondary cases. Long-term cure without surgery is rarely achieved.

faqs.question: What metabolic problems does pyloric stenosis cause?
faqs.answer: Repeated vomiting of gastric acid causes hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis with hypokalemia — low blood potassium and high blood pH. Severe hypokalemia can cause muscle weakness, cramps, and cardiac arrhythmias. Intravenous saline with potassium replacement corrects these abnormalities before surgery; operating on an uncorrected patient carries significant anesthetic risk.

faqs.question: Is adult pyloric stenosis related to stomach cancer?
faqs.answer: Gastric antral cancer can produce the same endoscopic and radiological appearance as hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. This is why multiple deep biopsies are mandatory in every adult case — a negative biopsy does not fully exclude submucosal cancer. CT imaging is also performed to look for lymph node involvement. Only after malignancy is excluded is benign surgical repair undertaken.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis after surgery for adult pyloric stenosis?
faqs.answer: Prognosis after pyloromyotomy or pyloroplasty is excellent for primary cases, with symptom resolution in 80-95% and low recurrence rates. Patients regain lost weight over two to four months. Secondary cases related to peptic ulcer disease do well if H. pylori is eradicated and acid suppression maintained. Untreated cases risk severe malnutrition and life-threatening electrolyte disturbances.

faqs.question: What is the succession splash sign?
faqs.answer: The succession splash is a sloshing or splashing sound heard when the clinician places a stethoscope on the abdomen and gently rocks the patient from side to side. It indicates that a large volume of fluid and air is retained in the stomach hours after the last meal — a sign of delayed gastric emptying from pyloric obstruction or gastroparesis. It is elicited more than four hours after eating for maximum significance.

faqs.question: Can adult pyloric stenosis be mistaken for an eating disorder?
faqs.answer: Yes, occasionally. Weight loss, food avoidance, and postprandial vomiting in a young adult may initially suggest an eating disorder. The key distinguishing features are objective gastric distension, a succession splash on examination, electrolyte abnormalities consistent with vomiting of gastric acid, and abnormal upper GI endoscopy or imaging showing mechanical obstruction.

faqs.question: How long is the hospital stay for pyloric stenosis surgery?
faqs.answer: Laparoscopic pyloromyotomy or pyloroplasty typically requires two to three days of hospitalization. Open procedures require three to five days. Pre-operative electrolyte correction adds one to three days. After discharge, diet advances from liquids to solids over two to four weeks under surgical guidance.

faqs.question: What foods are safe with pyloric stenosis before surgery?
faqs.answer: Liquid and semi-liquid foods pass through the narrowed pylorus more easily than solids. Soups, pureed foods, liquid nutritional supplements, and soft minced proteins are better tolerated. Avoid large portions, carbonated drinks during meals, and tough fibrous foods. Eat small amounts every two to three hours rather than three large meals.

faqs.question: Can pyloric stenosis recur after adult surgery?
faqs.answer: Recurrence is uncommon after successful pyloromyotomy or pyloroplasty for primary hypertrophic stenosis. Secondary cases linked to active peptic ulcer disease can recur if H. pylori is not eradicated and acid suppression is not maintained. Persistent symptoms after surgery warrant endoscopic assessment to confirm adequate pyloric opening and exclude incomplete myotomy.

faqs.question: Is adult pyloric stenosis hereditary?
faqs.answer: A genetic component is well established in infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis with familial clustering. Whether adult primary pyloric stenosis carries a similar hereditary basis is uncertain, given the extreme rarity and the absence of large family or genetic studies. Adults with a history of surgically corrected infant pyloric stenosis appear to have a small risk of recurrent stenosis later in life.

simpleName: Pyloric stenosis in adults (gastric outlet obstruction from pyloric hypertrophy)
metaTitle: Adult Pyloric Stenosis: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Adult hypertrophic pyloric stenosis: rare pyloric muscle thickening causing gastric outlet obstruction. Symptoms, surgery options and prognosis reviewed.

Okafor OI, Ayoade BA, Nnaji IC. 2010. Adult hypertrophic pyloric stenosis: a case report and review of literature. Nigerian Journal of Clinical Practice 13(3):348-350.
Holschneider AM, Ure BM. 2001. Infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. In: Stringer MD et al. (eds), Pediatric Surgery and Urology. Cambridge University Press.
Charan V, Memon MA. 1994. Adult hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. The American Journal of Gastroenterology 89(9):1620-1621.
Shone GR, Sherwood R, Brown P, et al. 1990. Adult pyloric stenosis. Postgraduate Medical Journal 66(782):1028-1030.
American Society for Gastrointestinal Endoscopy. 2012. The role of endoscopy in the management of benign pyloric stenosis. Gastrointestinal Endoscopy 76(4):741-744.
Malik A, Saxena A. 2001. Ultrasound in hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. Pediatric Radiology 31(3):145-150.
